title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering
authors: Vladimir Karpukhin, Barlas Oğuz, Sewon Min, Patrick Lewis et al.
hook: Google for facts, but with neural networks instead of just keywords.
promise: You will understand how dense retrieval transforms open-domain question answering from keyword to meaning-based search.
prerequisite_note: Bring an intuition for how search engines match questions to documents.

glossary.plain: A compact vector that captures the meaning of text for efficient comparison.
A model setup where questions and passages are separately encoded into vectors.
Answering questions using any passage in a large, unstructured text collection.
A popular keyword-based retrieval algorithm that counts word overlap and weights.
A numeric representation of text that preserves semantic meaning.
Finding short text chunks that are likely to contain an answer.

cards.title: From keywords to meaning
body: Learn how dense passage retrieval lets machines find answers by meaning, not just matching words.

cards.title: Why keyword search struggles
body: Imagine typing a question into a search engine and getting results that only match your exact words, missing the real meaning.
Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR) changes this by teaching computers to look for passages with the right meaning, even if the words are different.

cards.title: From question to answer: the dense route
body: First, a question and every passage in a huge collection are turned into vectors that capture meaning.
Then, the system searches for passages whose vectors are most similar to the question's vector, retrieving the best candidates.
cards.caption: DPR links questions to answers by comparing meaning, not just matching words.

cards.title: Keywords vs. meanings: which finds the answer?
body: Suppose you ask, 'Who wrote Hamlet?' and the passage says, 'Shakespeare is the author of Hamlet.'
cards.offNote: Might miss the passage if 'wrote' is not present.
cards.onNote: Finds the passage using learned meaning, even with different words.

cards.title: Training machines to understand
body: DPR uses two neural networks: one for questions, one for passages, each learning to turn text into a vector that captures meaning.
During training, it rewards the model for pulling matching question-passage pairs close together and pushing unrelated pairs apart.

cards.title: Accuracy vs. speed in retrieval
body: More accurate retrieval may require more computation, but too little can miss the answer.
cards.low: Quickly scans but may miss relevant passages.
cards.mid: Balances speed and recall, finds good candidates.
cards.high: Very thorough, but slower and more costly.

cards.title: What powers dense passage retrieval?
body: Pick the ingredients that make DPR work.

choices.label: Similarity search
choices.note: Finds which vectors are close in meaning.

choices.label: Contrastive training
choices.note: Teaches the model to pull matching pairs together.

choices.label: Large passage corpus
choices.note: Provides a huge set of possible answers.

choices.label: Fast vector index
choices.note: Allows efficient retrieval from millions of passages.

cards.title: Dense retrieval changes the game
body: DPR outperformed traditional keyword methods like BM25, retrieving more relevant passages for open-domain questions.
It set new benchmark-leading results on several QA benchmarks, showing the value of learned semantic search.
cards.caption: Dense retrieval finds more answers and does it efficiently at scale.

cards.title: A leap for question answering
body: Dense Passage Retrieval showed that neural models can search by meaning, not just words, making open-domain QA much more effective.
This approach laid the foundation for modern retrieval-augmented systems that blend large language models with fast, semantic search.

recap.label: Encode
recap.note: Turn questions and passages into dense vectors.

recap.label: Compare
recap.note: Measure similarity between question and passage vectors.

recap.label: Retrieve
recap.note: Select top passages matching the question's meaning.

recap.label: Answer
recap.note: Use retrieved passages to answer the question.

cards.title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering
body: Read the full paper for technical details and further experiments.
